1.lecture:
§1 · Why engineers script

A spreadsheet hides its logic inside cells; a script lays it out as readable text. That single difference buys the three things engineering calculation needs most: repeatability — run the script tomorrow on new data and the identical logic executes; auditability — a reviewer reads the calculation top to bottom, every assumption visible; and scale — the loop that processes one reading processes a million without new effort. Spreadsheets remain excellent scratchpads, but the moment a calculation matters, recurs, or grows, the professional tool is a script — and the habit of writing one is what this course installs.

§2 · The toolchain, plainly

Python is an interpreter: it reads statements and executes them immediately. You can feed it lines interactively — the REPL, ideal for trying an idea — or hand it a file of statements, a script, which is the durable product. A script is just text: openable in any editor, storable beside the data it processed, and — crucially — diffable, so yesterday's version and today's can be compared line by line. That property makes scripts the natural citizens of version control and the natural evidence in an engineering audit trail.

§3 · Anatomy of a calculation script

Every calculation script worth keeping has the same skeleton: named inputs at the top, with units in the names or comments (\( \texttt{flow\_m3h = 100} \)); the computation in clear steps, mirroring how you would write it on paper; and output that states units — \( \texttt{print(f"shaft power = \{P/1000:.1f\} kW")} \). Arithmetic operators behave as expected, with three worth flagging now: \( \texttt{**} \) is exponentiation, \( \texttt{/} \) always returns a decimal, and \( \texttt{//} \) divides and discards the remainder. A pump-power script in this shape is eight lines — and it replaces the back-of-envelope forever, because it IS the envelope, kept.

§4 · The scripting working method

Write scripts the way the mathematics course taught you to check units: as a discipline, not an afterthought. Name variables with their units — ambiguity between bar and kPa has sunk more calculations than any syntax error ever will. State inputs once, at the top, never buried mid-logic. Print results WITH units and sensible precision. Keep one calculation per script while learning; later lessons modularize. And before trusting any script, run it against a case you have verified by hand — the machine-verification habit this whole platform practices on you is the habit you are now learning to practice on your own code. A script that has never met a hand-checked case is an opinion with syntax.

Finally, keep scripts with their evidence. Store the script beside the data it processed and note the date and source of both — the calculation, its inputs, and its result then travel as one reviewable package. Months later, when a result is questioned, the answer is a rerun rather than an archaeology project; and when the data changes, the same package regenerates every downstream number identically. That closed loop — data, script, result, rerunnable — is what engineering computing means by an audit trail.
1.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Order-of-magnitude sense (Math I) — the sanity screen for every output.
  • Unit discipline (Math I, Physics I) — now encoded in variable names.
  • No prior programming — the course starts from zero, honestly.

Glossary

TermEquationWhen to use
Scriptstatements in a file, run top to bottomThe durable, auditable form of a calculation.
REPLtype a line, see the resultTrying ideas; never the final home of real work.
Variable\( \texttt{name = value} \)Named storage; put the unit in the name: \( \texttt{head\_m} \).
Operators\( \texttt{+ - * / // \%\ **} \)\( \texttt{/} \) gives decimals; \( \texttt{//} \) floors; \( \texttt{**} \) powers.
print with f-string\( \texttt{f"P = \{p:.1f\} kW"} \)Output that states value, precision, and unit together.
Comment\( \texttt{\#} \) to end of lineThe why beside the what; assumptions on the record.
Hand-check rulescript vs worked caseNo script is trusted before it reproduces a known answer.

quiz.q:
Trace the pump-power script

Problem. Trace this script and state its output:

rho = 1000        # kg/m3
g = 9.81          # m/s2
flow_m3h = 100
head_m = 30
eta = 0.70
Q = flow_m3h / 3600
P = rho * g * Q * head_m / eta
print(f"shaft power = {P/1000:.1f} kW")
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) \( Q = 100/3600 = 0.02778\ \mathrm{m^3/s} \). (2) \( P = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.02778 \times 30 / 0.70 = 11{,}679\ \mathrm{W} \). (3) Output: shaft power = 11.7 kW.

Answer: 11.7 kW. Sanity check: this is the exact calculation the mathematics course verified by hand — hydraulic 8.2 kW, divided by 0.7. The script is that envelope, kept: change the flow tomorrow and the identical logic reruns, which is the entire argument of §1.

quiz.q:
A conversion worth scripting

Problem. Write (and trace for 6.0 barg) a script converting gauge pressure in bar to absolute pressure in kPa: \( P_{abs} = P_{gauge} \times 100 + 101.325 \).
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) p_barg = 6.0; p_kpa_abs = p_barg * 100 + 101.325; print(f"{p_kpa_abs:.1f} kPa abs"). (2) Trace: \( 6.0 \times 100 + 101.325 = 701.3\ \mathrm{kPa\ abs} \).

Answer: 701.3 kPa absolute. Sanity check: the gauge trap from the mathematics course, now armored in code: the script's NAME says barg in and kPa-absolute out, so the assumption travels with the calculation — which is precisely what a cell formula never tells its reader.

quiz.q:
The running-cost one-liner

Problem. A 15 kW machine runs 16 h/day at an energy price of 0.045 per kWh. Script the daily cost and trace it.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) cost = 15 * 16 * 0.045. (2) \( 15 \times 16 = 240\ \mathrm{kWh/day} \); \( 240 \times 0.045 = 10.8 \) per day.

Answer: 10.8 currency units per day — ≈3,940 per year of continuous duty. Sanity check: kW times hours gives kWh (energy), times price-per-kWh gives money — the units chain cleanly, and the annualized figure (×365) is the number that turns a motor-efficiency conversation into a business case. Three inputs, one line, permanent tool.

quiz.q: The decisive advantage of a script over a spreadsheet for a recurring engineering calculation is:
choices: The logic is visible, reviewable, and reruns identically on new data
Scripts run faster, which is what matters
quiz.solution: Auditability and repeatability are the engineering virtues: every step readable top to bottom, every rerun identical. Scripts absolutely can contain errors — the hand-check rule exists because of it; speed rarely matters at calculation scale; and spreadsheets compute fine — they just hide the logic in cells where reviewers cannot see it whole.

quiz.q: In Python, 7 // 2 evaluates to:
quiz.solution: // is floor division: divide and discard the remainder — 3. Plain / gives 3.5 (always a float); rounding UP would give 4 (that's math.ceil); 1 is the REMAINDER, delivered by %. The three operators split the division jobs among themselves, and mixing them up is the first classic Python slip.

quiz.q: print(2 ** 3) outputs:
quiz.solution: ** is exponentiation: \( 2^3 = 8 \). The caret ^ — which many calculators use — means something entirely different in Python (bitwise XOR), and 2^3 would print 1: a legal expression computing the wrong thing silently, which is exactly the kind of error the hand-check rule is designed to catch.

quiz.q: The best variable name for a flow reading of 100 m³/h is:
quiz.solution: The unit rides in the name, so every later line that touches it knows what it holds — the script equivalent of the mathematics course's unit discipline. Single letters and generic names create the bar-versus-kPa ambiguities that sink calculations; six months later, flow_m3h still explains itself and x1 explains nothing.

quiz.q: Before a new calculation script is trusted, it should first:
choices: Reproduce a case already verified by hand
quiz.solution: Running without errors proves only that the syntax is legal — a script can be fluently, confidently wrong. The hand-checked case pins the LOGIC: if the script reproduces a known answer, its machinery is sound and may be pointed at unknown cases. Repetition of an unverified script just repeats the doubt. This is the platform's own machine-verification habit, handed to you.

2.lecture:
§1 · Types: what a value is

Every value in Python has a type, and the type decides what operations mean. Mechanics uses four constantly: int — whole numbers, exact; float — decimals, approximate by nature; str — text, including text that LOOKS like a number; and bool — True or False, the currency of decisions. The classic trap sits between str and float: data arrives from files and instruments as text, and "42.5" will not do arithmetic — "42.5" * 2 is not 85. Conversion is explicit: float("42.5") crosses the border, and crossing it consciously is the habit.

§2 · Floats tell the truth approximately

A float is a binary approximation of a decimal number, and the approximation shows: \( 0.1 + 0.2 \) is not exactly \( 0.3 \) in a computer, and testing equality between computed floats fails unpredictably. The professional pattern is the tolerance test — \( \texttt{abs(a - b) < 1e-9} \) — precisely the pattern every quiz verifier on this platform uses. Roundoff also accumulates: sum a million small floats and the last digits drift. None of this is a defect to fix; it is a property to respect, the numerical equivalent of instrument uncertainty.

§3 · Presenting numbers like an engineer

Computed values arrive with fifteen digits; engineering answers carry units and honest precision. The f-string is the tool: f"{p/1000:.2f} kW" renders 8175 W as \( \texttt{8.18 kW} \) — converted, rounded, and labeled in one expression. Precision in the format is a claim about accuracy, exactly as significant figures were in the physics course: print three decimals only if the calculation deserves them. The output line of a script is its public face; make it state value, precision, and unit together, every time.

§4 · The type-discipline working method

Treat every value's type as consciously as its unit. At the borders of the script — reading files, taking input — convert text to numbers explicitly and immediately, so the interior works entirely in numeric types. Compare floats only with tolerances; reserve equality for ints and strings. Name booleans as assertions — is_running, above_alarm — so conditions read as sentences. And when a result looks absurd, check types before logic: half the beginner's "impossible" outputs are a string where a number belonged, silently concatenated instead of added. The interpreter always does exactly what the types tell it to; the working method is making sure that is also what you meant.

Keep the int/float division of labor deliberate as well. Counts, indices, and loop variables are integers by nature — a plant has 14 pumps, never 14.0 — while every measured quantity is a float carrying its approximation honestly. Mixing them is legal (Python promotes to float), but REPORTING a count as a decimal reads as sloppiness, and rounding a float into an index without deciding between truncation and rounding is how off-by-one errors are born. When precision genuinely matters — currency totals, long accumulations — know that specialized decimal types exist and that the ordinary float is a measuring instrument, not a ledger. For this course's engineering arithmetic, floats with tolerance comparisons and honest formatting carry every calculation; the discipline is knowing which type each value IS, the same way the physics course always knew each quantity's unit — a habit checked once per variable and repaid on every line after.
2.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • The script skeleton (Lesson 1) — inputs, computation, labeled output.
  • Significant figures (Physics I) — precision as an honesty claim.
  • Tolerance thinking (Math I) — approximation with stated error.

Glossary

TermEquationWhen to use
int / floatexact whole vs approximate decimalCounts are ints; measurements are floats.
strtext, even when it looks numericFile and instrument data arrives as str — convert at the border.
boolTrue / FalseDecision currency; name them as assertions.
Conversion\( \texttt{float("42.5")},\ \texttt{int(x)} \)Explicit border crossings; int() truncates toward zero.
Float comparison\( \texttt{abs(a-b) < tol} \)Never == between computed floats.
f-string\( \texttt{f"\{v:.2f\} kW"} \)Value, precision, and unit in one expression.
Roundoffbinary approximation driftRespected like instrument uncertainty, not 'fixed'.

quiz.q:
The equality that fails

Problem. Predict the output of both lines, and write the correct comparison:

print(0.1 + 0.2 == 0.3)
print(abs((0.1 + 0.2) - 0.3) < 1e-9)
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) First line: False — in binary, 0.1 and 0.2 are repeating fractions; their sum is 0.30000000000000004. (2) Second line: True — the difference (≈\( 5.5\times10^{-17} \)) is far inside the \( 10^{-9} \) tolerance. (3) The tolerance test IS the correct float comparison.

Answer: False, then True. Sanity check: this is not a Python defect — every binary floating-point system on earth does it. Engineering already knows the answer: no two measured values are ever "equal" either, only equal within tolerance; the code pattern just says so explicitly.

quiz.q:
Crossing the text border

Problem. A level reading arrives from a file as the text "42.5". Show what "42.5" * 2 produces, and script the correct doubling.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) "42.5" * 2 → "42.542.5" — string repetition, perfectly legal, silently wrong. (2) Correct: reading = float("42.5"); doubled = reading * 2 → 85.0.

Answer: text repetition vs the numeric 85.0 — the conversion makes the difference. Sanity check: the wrong version raises no error at all — the type system did exactly what strings do. Every file-reading script in this course will convert at the border for exactly this reason; the habit costs one function call and prevents the silent classic.

quiz.q:
Publishing a result properly

Problem. A script computes p = 8175 (watts). Write the output line that reports it in kW to two decimals with its unit — and state exactly what prints.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) print(f"shaft power = {p/1000:.2f} kW"). (2) \( 8175/1000 = 8.175 \); format \( \texttt{.2f} \) rounds to two decimals. (3) Prints: shaft power = 8.18 kW.

Answer: shaft power = 8.18 kW. Sanity check: conversion, rounding, and labeling happened in ONE expression — nothing was stored rounded (the full 8175 remains available for further computation), which is the right division of labor: full precision inside, honest precision at the interface.

quiz.q: type("3.14") reports:
quiz.solution: Quotes make it text, whatever it resembles: "3.14" is a string of four characters. It becomes a number only through explicit conversion — float("3.14"). Instrument logs, CSV files, and user input all deliver strings; treating them as numbers without conversion is the most common silent error in beginner data scripts.

quiz.q: int(7.9) evaluates to:
quiz.solution: int() truncates toward zero — it drops the fraction, it does not round: 7. Rounding is a different, explicit request: round(7.9) gives 8. The distinction bites in bin-counting and indexing arithmetic, where a truncation assumed to be a rounding shifts everything by one. Choose the operation by name, not by hope.

quiz.q: Which comparison between two COMPUTED float values is professionally correct?
quiz.solution: Computed floats carry binary roundoff, so equality is a coin toss; the tolerance test states how close is close enough — an engineering judgment made explicit. String comparison inherits the same digits problem in costume, and is tests object identity, a different concept entirely. Every verifier on this platform uses the tolerance pattern; now you know why.

quiz.q: 10 / 4 evaluates to:
quiz.solution: Single slash is TRUE division and always returns a float: 2.5. Floor division 10 // 4 gives 2. (Older languages divided integers integrally, and imported habits from them cause exactly this surprise in reverse.) Knowing which division you asked for is part of type discipline — the operators are unambiguous even when memory isn't.

quiz.q: A boolean variable controlling an alarm branch is best named:
quiz.solution: Booleans are assertions, and naming them as assertions makes conditions read as sentences: if above_alarm: needs no comment. Generic names force every reader back up the script to discover what is being tested. (temp is doubly cursed — ambiguous between 'temporary' and 'temperature'.) Readable conditions are cheap insurance in any script an auditor may one day read.

3.lecture:
§1 · Decisions: if, elif, else

Programs earn their keep when behavior depends on data, and the instrument is the conditional. An if tests a boolean expression; elif chains further tests in order; else catches the remainder. Order matters and is a design decision: an alarm classifier tests the most severe threshold first, so a reading of 95 °C reports "trip" rather than the also-true "warning". Comparisons (\( \texttt{< <= == >= >} \)) and the boolean connectives (\( \texttt{and, or, not} \)) compose the tests; indentation — four spaces — is not decoration but syntax, defining exactly which lines each branch owns.

§2 · Repetition: for and while

The for loop walks a known collection — every reading in a list, every line in a file, every integer in range(n) (which yields 0 through \( n-1 \)) — and is the workhorse of data processing: initialize an accumulator, add each item, report. The while loop repeats as long as a condition holds and serves the open-ended questions: keep compounding the filter's pressure drop until it crosses the alarm line, and report how many weeks that took. Choose by the question's shape — known collection, for; unknown horizon, while — and give every while a guaranteed exit, because a condition that never turns false runs forever.

§3 · Guard clauses: refusing bad input early

Real calculations meet impossible inputs — negative flows, zero efficiencies, text where numbers belong — and the professional pattern rejects them at the door: if eta <= 0: raise ValueError("efficiency must be positive"). The guard clause converts a silent wrong answer into a loud, named failure at the exact line where the assumption broke. The mathematics course called this domain discipline — functions have domains, and \( \eta = 0 \) is not in this one; the guard writes the domain into the code.

§4 · The control-flow working method

Translate procedures the way you would explain them aloud. Write the decision table first — conditions in severity order, outcomes named — then transcribe it as an if/elif/else chain and test EVERY branch once, including the else. For loops, name the accumulator for what it accumulates (total_kwh, not s), initialize it visibly, and hand-trace the first two iterations on paper before trusting a thousand — the two-minute trace that catches off-by-one and wrong-order errors while they are still cheap. Guard first, compute second, report third: scripts organized in that order read as procedures, fail loudly at their borders, and hold up when the data turns hostile, which production data always eventually does.

Two loop hygienes complete the method. Keep the loop body honest about what it touches — a loop that both accumulates a total AND mutates the list it walks is two jobs tangled, and tangled jobs breed the subtle bugs; split them. And when a while-loop's exit depends on a computed quantity, add a hard iteration cap as a guard — if weeks > 1000: raise RuntimeError(...) — so a modeling mistake produces a named failure in milliseconds instead of a silent forever. Loops are where scripts spend their lives; discipline there is discipline everywhere.
3.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Booleans and comparisons (Lesson 2) — conditions are boolean expressions.
  • The exponential model (Math I) — today's while-loop example compounds it.
  • Named variables (Lesson 1) — accumulators with honest names.

Glossary

TermEquationWhen to use
if / elif / elsefirst true branch winsDecision chains; order encodes priority.
Comparison operators\( \texttt{< <= == >= > !=} \)The tests; == compares, = assigns — never confuse them.
for loop\( \texttt{for x in collection:} \)Known collections: readings, lines, range(n) = 0…n−1.
while loop\( \texttt{while condition:} \)Unknown horizons — with a guaranteed exit.
Accumulator patterninit, add per item, reportTotals, counts, maxima across any dataset.
Guard clause\( \texttt{if bad: raise ValueError(...)} \)Reject impossible inputs loudly, at the border.
breakexit the loop nowEarly exit when the answer is found.

quiz.q:
Trace the alarm classifier

Problem. Trace this classifier for readings of 85, then 96 (°C):

if t >= 95:
    level = "TRIP"
elif t >= 80:
    level = "ALARM"
elif t >= 70:
    level = "WARNING"
else:
    level = "NORMAL"
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) t = 85: the 95-test fails, the 80-test passes → "ALARM"; the 70-test is never reached. (2) t = 96: the FIRST test passes → "TRIP", chain exits immediately.

Answer: 85 → ALARM; 96 → TRIP. Sanity check: reverse the order — test 70 first — and a 96 °C reading would report "WARNING", since 96 ≥ 70 is also true and the first true branch wins. Severity-descending order is not style; it is the logic. This is why the working method writes the decision table before the code.

quiz.q:
The accumulator, traced

Problem. Trace the total across the loop:

readings = [4, 7, 2, 9]
total = 0
for r in readings:
    total = total + r
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) total starts 0. (2) After each pass: 0+4=4; 4+7=11; 11+2=13; 13+9=22. (3) Final: total = 22.

Answer: 22. Sanity check: hand-tracing the first two iterations (4, then 11) is the working method's cheap audit — if those match expectation, the pattern is sound for four items or four million. Forgetting the initialization line, the classic slip, would crash on the first pass rather than silently corrupt: Python's favor to beginners.

quiz.q:
How many weeks to the alarm?

Problem. A filter's ΔP starts at 0.30 bar and compounds 5% weekly; the alarm is 0.60 bar. Write the while loop that counts the weeks, and state its result.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) dp = 0.30; weeks = 0 while dp < 0.60: weeks += 1 dp = 0.30 * 1.05 ** weeks (2) Week 14: \( 0.30 \times 1.05^{14} = 0.594 \) — still under. Week 15: \( 0.624 \) — crossed. (3) Result: weeks = 15.

Answer: 15 weeks. Sanity check: the mathematics course's logarithm gave 14.2 weeks for the exact crossing — the loop answers the OPERATIONAL question (first whole week past the line), hence 15. Same physics, two honest answers to two subtly different questions; knowing which one you asked is the engineering.

quiz.q: range(5) iterates over:
quiz.solution: range(n) starts at 0 and stops BEFORE n: five values, 0 through 4. The zero-start convention aligns with list indexing (Lesson 6) so range(len(data)) walks every index — and the off-by-one instincts imported from 1-counting are the classic loop bug this fact retires. When 1 through 5 is genuinely wanted: range(1, 6).

quiz.q: The difference between = and == is:
quiz.solution: x = 5 stores; x == 5 asks. Python refuses plain assignment inside an if-condition, converting the classic typo into a visible syntax error — a kindness not all languages extend. Reading them aloud differently ('gets' versus 'equals') keeps the two straight in review, where the eye alone glides past a single missing character.

quiz.q: A guard clause like if eta <= 0: raise ValueError(...) exists to:
choices: Turn an impossible input into a loud, located failure instead of a silent wrong answer
quiz.solution: Fed η = 0, the unguarded script divides by zero somewhere downstream — or worse, a negative η produces a plausible-looking negative power that travels into a report. The guard names the broken assumption at the exact border where it entered. It is the code form of a domain statement, and auditors read guards as the script's declared operating envelope.

quiz.q: After total = 0 and for i in range(3): total += i, total equals:
quiz.solution: The loop adds 0, 1, 2 — sum 3. Answering 6 adds 1+2+3, the off-by-one imported from 1-counting; range's zero-start (previous question) decides it. The two-iteration hand trace — total: 0, then 1, then 3 — settles such questions in seconds, which is why the working method makes it a reflex before any loop is trusted at scale.

quiz.q: Inside a loop scanning readings for the first value above a limit, the statement that exits immediately upon finding it is:
quiz.solution: break leaves the loop at once — the found-it exit that saves scanning the rest. (continue, its sibling, skips to the next iteration instead.) There is no stop; killing the interpreter to end a loop is surgery with a hammer. Early exits also document intent: a reader sees the loop is a SEARCH, not a full sweep.

4.lecture:
§1 · Functions: calculations with names

A function packages a calculation behind a name and an interface: def shaft_power(q_m3s, head_m, eta=0.7): declares what goes in, return declares what comes out, and everything between is the calculation, written once and reused forever. The docstring — a quoted paragraph directly under the def — states what the function does AND its units, which turns the function into a self-documenting engineering tool: help(shaft_power) answers the reviewer before the reviewer asks. Default arguments (the eta=0.7) encode the usual case while keeping the unusual case one keyword away.

§2 · Return, not print

A function that PRINTS its result has spent it; a function that RETURNS it hands the value to the caller for further use — feeding the next calculation, a comparison, a plot, a test. Printing is presentation, the script's outer layer; returning is plumbing, the composable interior. The distinction becomes visible the first time you try to use a printed result in arithmetic and find you hold None. Compose functions the way the chain rule composed rates: small functions, each doing one nameable thing, wired by their returns — \( \texttt{gap(c\_to\_k(90), c\_to\_k(30))} \) reads like the sentence it implements.

§3 · Scope: what happens in a function stays there

Variables born inside a function are local: invisible outside, gone when the call ends. This is a feature — ten functions can each use x without collision — and a discipline: a function should receive what it needs as arguments and deliver what it produces as returns, never reaching sideways into the script's globals. Functions written that way are testable in isolation, which is exactly how the next lesson will test them.

§4 · The function-design working method

Extract a function whenever a calculation is used twice, or once but nameable. Name it by what it returns (hydraulic_power_w), carry units in parameter names, and write the docstring FIRST — if you cannot state the contract in a sentence, the function is not yet one thing. Keep functions small enough to hand-verify: one formula, one decision, one transformation each. Then apply Lesson 1's rule at the function scale: every function meets a hand-checked case before it is trusted, and the check lives beside the function as next lesson's assertion. A personal library of such functions — built, checked, and reused across this course — is the deliverable this working method is quietly assembling.

Resist the two classic function pathologies while the habits are forming. The do-everything function — read the file, convert the units, compute the power, print the report — cannot be tested except by running the world; split it at every nameable seam and each piece becomes checkable alone. And the copy-paste twin — the same formula pasted in three places with one variable renamed — guarantees that a future correction reaches two of the three; extraction into one function makes the fix land everywhere by construction. Functions are the unit of reuse, of testing, and of trust: size them so all three work.
4.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • The script skeleton (Lessons 1–3) — functions modularize it.
  • Function notation (Math I) — f(x) becomes def f(x):.
  • Unit-bearing names (Lesson 2) — now on parameters too.

Glossary

TermEquationWhen to use
def / return\( \texttt{def f(x): ... return y} \)The named, reusable calculation with a declared interface.
Docstringcontract + units, under the defThe function's datasheet; help() reads it back.
Default argument\( \texttt{eta=0.7} \)The usual case built in; override by keyword when unusual.
Return vs printplumbing vs presentationReturn feeds further computation; print spends the value.
Local scopeinside variables die at returnIsolation that makes functions testable and reusable.
Composition\( \texttt{g(f(x))} \)Small functions wired by returns — the chain rule's cousin.
One-thing ruleone formula / decision per functionSmall enough to hand-verify is small enough to trust.

quiz.q:
Build and trace the power function

Problem. Trace the call shaft_power(0.025, 25):

def shaft_power(q_m3s, head_m, eta=0.7):
    """Shaft power in W for water. q in m3/s, head in m."""
    p_hyd = 1000 * 9.81 * q_m3s * head_m
    return p_hyd / eta
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) \( p_{hyd} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.025 \times 25 = 6131\ \mathrm{W} \). (2) η defaults to 0.7: \( 6131/0.7 = 8759\ \mathrm{W} \). (3) Returns ≈8.76 kW (the value — nothing prints unless the caller prints).

Answer: ≈8759 W returned. Sanity check: the default carried the usual efficiency silently; a stiffer pump calls shaft_power(0.025, 25, eta=0.8) → 7664 W. One function now serves every pump problem this course has computed by hand — which is precisely the point of writing it.

quiz.q:
Three statistics, one return

Problem. Write a function returning the minimum, maximum, and mean of a list, and trace it on [3, 9, 6].
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) def stats(xs): return min(xs), max(xs), sum(xs)/len(xs) (2) Trace: min 3, max 9, mean \( 18/3 = 6.0 \). (3) Caller unpacks: lo, hi, avg = stats(readings).

Answer: \( (3,\ 9,\ 6.0) \). Sanity check: the mean lies between min and max — the free ordering audit every statistics return should pass; and returning the tuple (rather than printing three lines) leaves the caller free to alarm on hi, trend avg, or ignore lo: plumbing, not presentation.

quiz.q:
Composed conversions

Problem. Given def c_to_k(t_c): return t_c + 273.15 and def gap(a, b): return a - b, trace gap(c_to_k(90), c_to_k(30)) and comment on the design.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Inner calls first: \( 90 + 273.15 = 363.15 \); \( 30 + 273.15 = 303.15 \). (2) gap(363.15, 303.15) → 60.0 K.

Answer: 60.0 K. Sanity check: a temperature DIFFERENCE is identical in °C and K — the offsets cancel, and the composition proves it numerically. Two tiny, individually verifiable functions composed by returns produced a correct result neither prints: composition is how small trusted pieces build large trusted calculations.

quiz.q: A function's docstring should state, at minimum:
choices: What it computes and the units of inputs and output
quiz.solution: The docstring is the function's datasheet: contract plus units, readable via help() without opening the source. Authorship and dates belong to version control, which tracks them automatically. 'Self-documenting code' covers WHAT lines do, never which unit a parameter expects — and unit ambiguity, as ever, is the error that costs the most and announces itself the least.

quiz.q: A function that prints its result instead of returning it:
choices: Cannot feed its value into further computation
quiz.solution: Printing sends characters to the screen and returns None — try arithmetic on it and the error arrives one line later, mystifying beginners. Returning hands the caller a value to compute with, test, or print AT THE EDGE of the script. The rule of thumb: functions return; the script's last lines print. Everything composable follows from it.

quiz.q: A variable created inside a function is, outside the function:
choices: Invisible — it exists only during the call
quiz.solution: Local scope: born at the call, gone at the return. This isolation is what lets ten functions use x without collision, and what makes each testable alone. Needing a function's inner value outside is the signal to RETURN it — reaching in (or leaking globals out) couples everything to everything, and coupled scripts break in sympathy.

quiz.q: With def f(x): return x * x, the value of f(f(2)) is:
quiz.solution: Inside-out: f(2) = 4, then f(4) = 16. Composition evaluates inner calls first — exactly the chain-rule reading order from the mathematics course. Answering 8 computes \( 2^3 \), a different composition; 4 stops after one call. Tracing nested calls inner-first is a two-second discipline that scales to arbitrarily deep pipelines.

quiz.q: In def shaft_power(q_m3s, head_m, eta=0.7), the eta=0.7 means:
choices: Callers may omit eta and get 0.7; or pass their own
Eta must always be supplied as 0.7
The function only works at 70% efficiency
quiz.solution: A default argument encodes the usual case without forbidding the unusual: shaft_power(q, h) uses 0.7; shaft_power(q, h, eta=0.82) overrides it. Defaults document typical practice right in the signature — and keep call sites short where the typical applies. The pattern recurs across every engineering library you will ever import.

5.lecture:
§1 · Why engineers test code

This platform machine-verifies every numeric answer before publishing it; this lesson hands you the same discipline for your own scripts. A test is a claim made executable: run the function on a case whose answer you know, and let the computer confirm agreement — today, tomorrow, and after every future edit. Untested code decays silently: an innocent change to one line shifts a result three functions away, and without tests nobody notices until the wrong number is in a report. With tests, the same mistake fails loudly in seconds, at your desk, before it costs anything.

§2 · The assertion: a claim with teeth

The core tool is one statement: assert condition, "message" — silent when the condition holds, a named failure when it breaks. For engineering functions the pattern is the tolerance form from Lesson 2: assert abs(area_m2(0.1) - 0.007854) < 1e-6 — the hand-checked case (a 100 mm circle's \( \pi d^2/4 \)) pinned beside the function it certifies. Three or four assertions per function — a typical case, an edge (zero, the domain boundary), and a case exercising each branch — form a small permanent test suite that reruns in milliseconds. The habit converts "I checked it once when I wrote it" into "it is checked on every run", which is the difference between a memory and a guarantee.

§3 · Exceptions and honest failure

Guard clauses (Lesson 3) raise exceptions at broken assumptions; testing meets them from the other side. A function fed garbage should fail LOUDLY — ValueError with a message naming the violated assumption — never return a plausible wrong number. Debugging itself is hypothesis testing, the scientific method at a keyboard: reproduce the failure with the smallest possible input, form a hypothesis about the earliest wrong value, place a print or assertion just before the suspect line, and bisect until the bug has nowhere to hide. Staring is not a method; bisection is.

§4 · The verification working method

Adopt the platform's own pipeline at personal scale. Write the function; write its hand-checked case as an assertion immediately — while the paper calculation is still on the desk; add the edge-case and branch assertions; keep the whole block at the bottom of the file or in a test script, and run it after EVERY change. When a bug surfaces in use, its fix begins by writing the failing case as a new assertion — so the same bug can never return unannounced. And respect the honest limit: tests prove the presence of agreement with your checked cases, not the absence of all error — which is precisely why the cases are chosen with engineering judgment: the typical, the extreme, and the branch nobody believes will ever run. That judgment is the engineering; the assertions merely keep it enforced. A dozen well-chosen assertions guarding a personal library of checked functions is a small thing to maintain and a large thing to own: it is the difference between code you wrote and code you can vouch for.
5.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Functions (Lesson 4) — the unit under test.
  • Tolerance comparison (Lesson 2) — the float-safe assertion form.
  • Guard clauses (Lesson 3) — loud failure at broken assumptions.

Glossary

TermEquationWhen to use
assert\( \texttt{assert cond, "msg"} \)A claim with teeth: silent if true, named failure if not.
Tolerance assertion\( \texttt{assert abs(f(x)-known) < tol} \)The float-safe test against a hand-checked case.
Edge casezero, boundary, emptyWhere functions break first; always among the test cases.
ValueError\( \texttt{raise ValueError(...)} \)Refusing garbage loudly instead of returning plausible nonsense.
Regression testyesterday's bug as today's assertionFixed bugs stay fixed, by construction.
Bisection debugginghalve the search each probeLocating the earliest wrong value systematically.
Test-first habitassertion written with the functionWhile the hand-checked case is still on the desk.

quiz.q:
Pin the area function

Problem. Given def area_m2(d_m): return 3.14159 * d_m**2 / 4, write the tolerance assertion pinning it to the hand-checked case d = 0.1 m, and state whether it passes.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Hand check: \( A = \pi d^2/4 = \pi \times 0.01/4 = 0.007854\ \mathrm{m^2} \). (2) Assertion: assert abs(area_m2(0.1) - 0.007854) < 1e-6. (3) The function's 5-digit π gives 0.0078540 — inside tolerance: PASSES.

Answer: the assertion passes; the function is pinned. Sanity check: the tolerance \( 10^{-6} \) was chosen to admit the 5-digit π but reject real errors — a factor-of-4 slip or a missing square lands orders of magnitude away. Tolerances are engineering judgments: tight enough to catch bugs, loose enough to ignore roundoff.

quiz.q:
Find the bug by its units

Problem. This function claims to return lifting power in watts, but its test fails. Locate the bug and fix it:

def lift_power_w(mass_kg, height_m, time_s):
    """Average power to lift mass through height in given time."""
    return mass_kg * 9.81 * height_m

assert abs(lift_power_w(400, 12, 60) - 784.8) < 0.1
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) The return line computes \( mgh \) — ENERGY (47,088 J), never dividing by time: the units are joules, not watts. (2) Fix: return mass_kg * 9.81 * height_m / time_s. (3) Check: \( 400 \times 9.81 \times 12/60 = 784.8\ \mathrm{W} \) — the assertion passes.

Answer: missing / time_s; corrected, the test passes. Sanity check: the physics course's energy-versus-power distinction caught a software bug — the failing assertion pointed at it, and the unit audit named it. This is the standard rhythm: the test detects, the engineering diagnoses.

quiz.q:
Guard, then prove the guard

Problem. Extend shaft_power with a guard against non-positive efficiency, and write the test that PROVES the guard fires.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Guard: if eta <= 0: raise ValueError("eta must be positive") as the function's first line. (2) Proving it: try: call with eta=0; if no exception arrives, assert False, "guard did not fire"; in the except ValueError: branch, pass. (3) Now BOTH behaviors are pinned: right answers for good input, loud refusal for bad.

Answer: guard + try/except test, both permanent. Sanity check: testing that failure HAPPENS feels odd once — then obvious: the guard is a promised behavior like any other, and an accidental deletion of it should fail the suite. Safety systems are tested by attempting what they must prevent; code guards earn the same respect.

quiz.q: An assertion beside a function buys you, above a one-time manual check:
choices: Automatic re-verification on every future run and edit
quiz.solution: The manual check evaporates the moment it scrolls off screen; the assertion re-executes it forever — especially after edits, when yesterday's correctness is most quietly at risk. Tests never prove the absence of all error (the cases are finite), and they cost lines and microseconds — a spectacular exchange rate for the guarantee they do provide.

quiz.q: The most valuable additional test cases for flow_velocity(q, d) beyond the typical case are:
choices: Edge cases — zero flow, and the smallest/largest realistic diameter
More typical cases with similar numbers
quiz.solution: Functions break at their edges: zero triggers divisions and log domains, extremes expose unit and overflow assumptions. Five similar mid-range cases re-test the same path five times; edges test the paths that fail in production at 3 a.m. Random inputs without known answers verify nothing — a test needs an expected value to compare against.

quiz.q: Fed an impossible input, a professional calculation function should:
choices: Raise a named exception explaining the violated assumption
quiz.solution: A returned 0 or guess is a plausible number that travels — into comparisons, reports, decisions — with nothing marking it poisoned. The exception stops the pipeline at the exact border where the assumption broke, with a message naming it. Printed warnings scroll past unread in any automated run. Loud early failure is cheap; quiet wrong numbers compound.

quiz.q: The systematic way to locate a bug in a 40-line calculation is:
choices: Check intermediate values at the midpoint and bisect toward the earliest wrong one
quiz.solution: Bisection: probe the middle — if values are right there, the bug is downstream; wrong, upstream. Each probe halves the search; 40 lines fall in ~6 probes. Rereading finds what you expect to see (which is how the bug survived writing); rewriting risks a new bug and loses the lesson; blanket try/except SILENCES failures — the opposite of locating them.

quiz.q: After fixing a bug, the professional final step is:
choices: Add the failing case as a permanent assertion
quiz.solution: The regression test: the input that exposed the bug becomes a permanent claim, so the same failure can never return unannounced — including via some future 'improvement' that reintroduces it. Commented-out code is noise that rots; 'it's fixed' is true only until the next edit. Suites grow by exactly this route: every scar becomes armor.

6.lecture:
§1 · Lists: ordered data with a name

Engineering data arrives in sequences — readings over a route, temperatures over a shift — and the list is Python's sequence: readings = [2.1, 4.5, 3.2], ordered, indexable, growable. Indexing starts at ZERO — readings[0] is the first item — and negative indices count from the end, readings[-1] being the latest reading, an idiom worth owning immediately. Slices carve subranges (readings[1:3] yields items 1 and 2 — the stop index is excluded, mirroring range), append grows the list in place, and the built-ins len, sum, min, max answer the first questions any dataset is asked. The list comprehension compresses the transform loop to a phrase — [x * 0.746 for x in hp_values] — and reads like the sentence it implements.

§2 · Dictionaries: records with named fields

Where lists order items, dictionaries name them: pump = {"tag": "P-101", "power_kw": 11.5} is an equipment record whose fields are looked up by key — pump["power_kw"] — not by position. Adding a field is one assignment; asking whether one exists is "tag" in pump. The two structures compose into the universal data shape of plant work: a LIST of DICTIONARIES — one dict per equipment item, one list per fleet — which is exactly what a CSV file of records becomes the moment Python reads it.

§3 · Files: where real data lives

The CSV — comma-separated values, one record per line — is the lingua franca of every historian export, test-bench log, and maintenance system dump. Reading one is three idioms stacked: iterate the file line by line, split(",") each line into fields, and convert numeric fields with float() at the border, exactly as Lesson 2 demanded. The with open(...) as f: form opens the file and guarantees it closes. Everything after the read is Lessons 3–4: loops, accumulators, and functions, now fed by real data instead of typed constants.

§4 · The data-structure working method

Choose the structure by the question the data answers. Ordered measurements → list; named fields of one thing → dict; a fleet of records → list of dicts; and when you catch yourself tracking parallel lists (tags in one, powers in another), that is the signal the records want to be dicts. Convert types at the file border, never downstream. Trust the built-ins before writing loops — sum, max, len are tested code you did not have to write. And hand-trace list operations on a three-item example before applying them to thirty thousand rows, exactly as the loop lesson traced its accumulators: the small case proves the pattern, the large case merely repeats it.

One caution as datasets grow: a structure choice made casually at three records becomes architecture at thirty thousand. The list-of-dicts shape scales gracefully because every operation this course teaches — loops, comprehensions, filters, summaries — walks it unchanged; ad-hoc shapes (nested lists of mixed meaning, parallel lists) accumulate special cases until the script is mostly exceptions. Choose the boring, regular shape early and the analysis lessons ahead inherit clean footing.
6.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Loops and accumulators (Lesson 3) — now walking real collections.
  • Type conversion at borders (Lesson 2) — float() on every numeric field.
  • Functions (Lesson 4) — file-readers and summarizers with names.

Glossary

TermEquationWhen to use
List\( \texttt{[2.1, 4.5, 3.2]} \)Ordered, growable sequences of measurements.
Zero-based index\( \texttt{a[0]} \) first, \( \texttt{a[-1]} \) lastThe counting convention behind every off-by-one.
Slice\( \texttt{a[1:3]} \) — stop excludedSubranges; start included, stop excluded, like range.
Comprehension\( \texttt{[f(x) for x in xs]} \)The transform loop as a readable phrase.
Dictionary\( \texttt{\{"tag": "P-101"\}} \)Named fields, looked up by key — the record.
List of dictsone dict per recordThe fleet shape; what CSVs become in memory.
CSV reading\( \texttt{split(",")} \) + \( \texttt{float()} \)Line → fields → numbers, converted at the border.

quiz.q:
The route list, operated

Problem. Trace: start with readings = [2.1, 4.5, 3.2]; append 5.0; report the last element, the maximum, and the length.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) After readings.append(5.0): [2.1, 4.5, 3.2, 5.0]. (2) readings[-1] → 5.0 (the newest); max(readings) → 5.0; len(readings) → 4.

Answer: 5.0, 5.0, 4. Sanity check: the newest reading happening to BE the maximum is the vibration-route bad news pattern — and readings[-1] == max(readings) is exactly the one-line trend alarm a later script will build from these three idioms. Small operations, real diagnostics.

quiz.q:
An equipment record that grows

Problem. Build the record pump = {"tag": "P-101", "power_kw": 11.5}; add a field "status": "running"; read the power; and show the fleet shape for two pumps.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Add: pump["status"] = "running" — dicts grow by assignment. (2) Read: pump["power_kw"] → 11.5. (3) Fleet: fleet = [{"tag": "P-101", "power_kw": 11.5}, {"tag": "P-102", "power_kw": 7.5}] — a list of dicts, iterated as for pump in fleet:.

Answer: field added, 11.5 read, fleet as list-of-dicts. Sanity check: the alternative — parallel lists of tags and powers — breaks the moment one list is sorted or filtered without the other; the record keeps each pump's facts fused. This shape IS the maintenance database row, and recognizing it is the lesson.

quiz.q:
Reading the export

Problem. A file contains two lines: P-101,11.5 and P-102,7.5. Script the total power and trace it.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) total = 0.0 with open("pumps.csv") as f: for line in f: fields = line.split(",") total += float(fields[1]) (2) Trace: 0.0 + 11.5 = 11.5; 11.5 + 7.5 = 19.0.

Answer: 19.0 kW total. Sanity check: every piece is a prior lesson — the with-open border, the split into fields, the float() conversion (without which += would crash on a string), the accumulator. File processing is not a new skill; it is the old skills fed by a file, which is why the course built them first.

quiz.q: For a = [10, 20, 30, 40], the value of a[1] is:
quiz.solution: Zero-based indexing: a[0] is 10, so a[1] is 20 — the SECOND item. The 1-counting instinct from everyday life is the source of most beginner index errors, and range(n)'s 0-to-n−1 convention (Lesson 3) was designed to match this exactly: a[i] for i in range(len(a)) touches every element with no off-by-one anywhere.

quiz.q: The idiomatic way to read the LAST element of list a is:
quiz.solution: Negative indices count from the end: a[-1] is the last, a[-2] the one before. a[len(a)] is one PAST the end — an IndexError, and the classic fencepost slip (the valid last index is len(a)−1). The other forms belong to other languages. For time-ordered data, a[-1] is 'the latest reading' — an idiom used daily.

quiz.q: [x * 2 for x in [1, 2, 3]] evaluates to:
quiz.solution: The comprehension applies the expression to each element: doubles, collected in order. Repetition ([1,2,3,1,2,3]) is what [1,2,3] * 2 does — the list-level operator, a different animal; squares would need x ** 2. Comprehensions are the transform loop compressed to a readable phrase, and they appear in nearly every data script from here on.

quiz.q: Field values pulled from a CSV line by split(",") arrive as:
choices: Strings — numeric fields need float() before arithmetic
Floats if they contain a decimal point
quiz.solution: Files are text; split returns strings, whatever they resemble — and "11.5" + "7.5" concatenates to "11.57.5" rather than adding. CSV declares no types; nothing converts automatically. The border-conversion rule of Lesson 2 exists precisely for this moment: float() each numeric field at the read, and the interior of the script stays safely numeric.

quiz.q: Tracking pump tags in one list and their powers in a parallel list becomes fragile because:
choices: Sorting or filtering one list without the other silently misaligns every record
Lists cannot hold strings and floats
quiz.solution: Parallel lists are coupled by nothing but hope: one sort, one filter, one insertion applied to only one of them, and every tag points at the wrong power — silently. The list-of-dicts shape fuses each record's fields so they travel together through any operation. Memory and speed are irrelevant at this scale; integrity is the argument, and it is decisive.

7.lecture:
§1 · The array idea: operate on all of it

Lists process element by element through loops; numerical arrays (NumPy's ndarray) operate on whole datasets at once: kw = hp * 0.746 converts every horsepower value in one expression — vectorization. The gains are two: the code READS as the mathematics (one formula, visibly applied to everything), and it runs orders of magnitude faster, because the looping happens in optimized machinery below. For engineering data — a year of hourly readings is 8,760 numbers, a vibration waveform 65,536 — the array is the natural container, and the loop becomes the exception rather than the habit.

§2 · The reductions: from many numbers to a few

Analysis usually ends in reductions: mean, std, min/max, sum — the descriptive statistics of a dataset in one call each. The standard deviation deserves engineering respect: it is the honest width of the scatter, the number that turns "about 4.25" into "4.25 ± 0.30", and the seed of every control chart the metrology course will draw. Alongside them, argmax answers WHERE — the index of the worst reading — which is often the more actionable question: not "how bad" but "which machine".

§3 · Masks: asking questions of data

Comparison on an array yields an array of booleans — a mask — and the mask selects: readings[readings > 4.0] is every reading above the alarm line, in one expression: no loop, no if, no accumulator. Count them with sum of the mask, average them, or feed them onward. The idiom — condition, mask, selection — is the data-analysis sentence structure, and it compounds: masks combine with \( \texttt{\&} \) and \( \texttt{|} \) into compound questions ("above alarm AND after the overhaul") that still read as one line.

§4 · The vectorized working method

Reach for the array whenever data is homogeneous and numeric; keep lists for mixed records. Write transformations as whole-array expressions and CHECK them on a three-value array first — vectorized code is compact enough that a wrong formula corrupts everything uniformly, so the small trace matters doubly. Prefer the built-in reductions to hand-rolled loops (they are tested code, and faster), and when a reduction's answer surprises, look at the mask counts before doubting the data: how many values, how many above threshold, how many NaN — the three questions that catch most data-quality issues at the door. Then the discipline that spans every lesson: units ride the variable names (vel_mm_s), conversions happen once, visibly, at the border, and any result consumed downstream gets an assertion pinning a known case. Arrays change the scale of what one line does; they change nothing about what makes the line trustworthy.

The payoff arrives immediately in the lessons ahead: plotting consumes arrays whole, curve fitting reduces them to parameters, and simulations generate them by the thousand. Vectorized thinking — state the operation once, apply it to everything, summarize with a reduction — is also precisely how plant historians and analysis platforms work internally, so fluency here reads directly onto the tools the later automation courses will operate.
7.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Lists and loops (Lessons 3, 6) — arrays replace the transform loop.
  • Statistics vocabulary — mean and standard deviation as scatter's width.
  • Assertions (Lesson 5) — vectorized code gets pinned like any other.

Glossary

TermEquationWhen to use
Array (ndarray)homogeneous numeric blockThe natural container for measurements at scale.
Vectorization\( \texttt{kw = hp * 0.746} \)One expression, every element; reads as the mathematics.
Reductions\( \texttt{mean, std, min, max, sum} \)Whole datasets to summary numbers, one call each.
Standard deviationscatter's honest widthThe ± that belongs beside every reported mean.
argmax / argminindex of the extremeWHICH machine, not just how bad.
Mask\( \texttt{a[a > 4.0]} \)Condition → boolean array → selection, in one line.
Compound masks\( \texttt{(a>4) \& (t>t0)} \)Multi-condition questions that still read as sentences.

quiz.q:
Fleet conversion in one line

Problem. Motor ratings arrive in horsepower: [10, 25, 50]. Convert the whole set to kW (1 hp = 0.746 kW) vectorized, and state the result.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) hp = np.array([10, 25, 50]); kw = hp * 0.746. (2) Result: [7.46, 18.65, 37.3] — every element converted by the one expression.

Answer: [7.46, 18.65, 37.3] kW. Sanity check: the same operation on a plain LIST — [10,25,50] * 0.746 — is an error, and * 3 would have REPEATED the list: the array's element-wise arithmetic is a genuinely different contract, and knowing which container you hold decides what * means.

quiz.q:
Mean and scatter of a survey

Problem. Four bearing-temperature readings: 4.1, 3.9, 4.3, 4.7 (°C above ambient). Find: the mean and the (population) standard deviation, and report them together.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Mean: \( (4.1+3.9+4.3+4.7)/4 = 4.25 \). (2) Deviations: −0.15, −0.35, 0.05, 0.45; squares sum to 0.35; variance \( 0.35/4 = 0.0875 \). (3) \( \sigma = \sqrt{0.0875} = 0.30 \). Report: \( 4.25 \pm 0.30 \) °C.

Answer: mean 4.25, σ ≈ 0.30 — "4.25 ± 0.30 °C". Sanity check: σ has the data's own units and sits comfortably inside the observed spread (range 0.8) — a σ larger than the range would announce an arithmetic slip. The ± habit turns every reported mean into an honest claim, and the metrology course will build control charts on exactly this number.

quiz.q:
The alarm-line question

Problem. Vibration readings [3.2, 5.1, 2.8, 6.0] mm/s; the alert line is 4.0. Using a mask, find how many readings exceed it and their average.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Mask: v > 4.0 → [False, True, False, True]. (2) Count: 2. Selection: v[v > 4.0] → [5.1, 6.0]. (3) Mean of offenders: \( (5.1+6.0)/2 = 5.55\ \mathrm{mm/s} \).

Answer: 2 readings above the line, averaging 5.55 mm/s. Sanity check: condition → mask → selection replaced a four-line loop-with-if, and reads as the question itself. Scale it to a year of route data and the same one-liner is a fleet exception report — the entire reason the mask idiom is worth owning.

quiz.q: np.array([1, 2, 3]) * 2 evaluates to:
quiz.solution: Arrays do element-wise arithmetic: every value doubles. The repetition answer is what a plain LIST does with * 2 — the container decides the contract, and this pair of behaviors side by side is the cleanest illustration of why. Knowing which type you hold (Lesson 2's discipline, scaled up) is what makes the one-character operator safe.

quiz.q: argmax returns:
choices: The INDEX of the largest value
quiz.solution: max answers 'how bad'; argmax answers 'WHERE' — the position, which maps to a machine, a timestamp, a route point. In maintenance data the location is usually the actionable half: tags[np.argmax(vibration)] names the machine to visit. The pairing of a value-question with its index-question runs through all of applied data work.

quiz.q: The standard deviation of a set of readings tells you:
choices: The typical spread of readings around their mean, in the data's own units
quiz.solution: σ is scatter's width — the honest ± beside the mean. It bounds nothing absolutely (readings beyond σ are routine — most data holds ~1/3 of it there), says nothing about BIAS (a precisely wrong instrument scatters tightly around the wrong value), and instrument accuracy is a calibration question. Reporting mean ± σ claims exactly what the data supports, no more.

quiz.q: A vectorized formula applied to 30,000 readings should first be checked by:
choices: Running it on a tiny array with a hand-computable answer
Running it twice on the full data
Checking that no error is raised
quiz.solution: Vectorization's power is uniform application — which means a wrong formula corrupts all 30,000 values uniformly and plausibly. The three-value trace with a known answer pins the formula; then scale merely repeats proven arithmetic. Re-running identical wrongness reproduces it identically; absence of errors proves syntax, not sense — the recurring theme every verification lesson has hammered.

quiz.q: For a = np.array([10, 20, 30, 40]), the slice a[1:3] is:
quiz.solution: Same convention as lists and range: start included, stop excluded — indices 1 and 2, values 20 and 30. The consistency across list, range, and array slicing is deliberate: one fencepost rule serves the whole language, and internalizing 'stop is excluded' once retires the off-by-one everywhere at once.

8.lecture:
§1 · The plot is an argument

An engineering plot exists to support a decision, and its anatomy is the argument's grammar: axes labeled WITH UNITS (an unlabeled axis is a number with no meaning — the chart version of the unit sins every course here prosecutes), a title that states what is shown, and a legend when more than one series appears. The choice of plot type is the choice of claim: a LINE plot claims continuity — readings ordered in time, trends; a SCATTER plot claims only "these pairs were observed" — right for correlation questions and unordered data; a BAR chart compares discrete categories — machines, months, failure modes. Choosing a line where a scatter belongs invents continuity the data never promised; the plot type is the first honesty decision.

§2 · Scales that reveal

Linear axes hide what logarithmic axes reveal. Exponential decay — the cooling curves and filter-loading trends of the mathematics course — plots as a curve on linear axes but as a STRAIGHT LINE on a semilog (log-y) plot, whose slope reads the time constant: plotting \( \ln(T - T_\infty) \) against t was exactly this move, done by hand. Data spanning decades of magnitude — vibration spectra, particle counts — needs log scales simply to be seen: on a linear axis, the small values flatten into the floor. The working question before every plot: does this data live on one scale, or several?

§3 · Sampling honesty

Plotted data was SAMPLED, and sampling has physics. A signal changing faster than half the sampling rate does not disappear — it ALIASES, reappearing as a false slow signal: a 50 Hz vibration sampled at 60 Hz shows up as a phantom 10 Hz wobble (\( |50 - 60| \)). The instrumentation course will formalize Nyquist; the plotting lesson's version is the caution: before trusting a surprising slow oscillation in sampled data, ask what the sampling rate was. Equally, decimating data for plotting speed (every tenth point) is legitimate — STATED; silently, it is how spikes vanish from trend reviews.

§4 · The plotting working method

Build every plot in the same order. State the question the plot must answer; choose the type by the claim (trend → line, correlation → scatter, comparison → bar); choose scales by the data's structure (exponential suspicion → semilog; decades of range → log); label axes with quantities AND units; then read the plot yourself before showing anyone — does it actually answer the question, and would a skeptic's eye find the axis tricks (truncated baselines, cherry-picked windows) that make weak cases look strong? The mathematics course called the plot the test of first-order behavior; this lesson generalizes: the plot is where data confesses its structure, provided the plotter asks honestly. A figure that survives your own skepticism is ready for the morning meeting — and a figure that decided a question honestly is worth saving beside the script and data that produced it, completing the audit trail this course keeps building.
8.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Arrays (Lesson 7) — plots consume them directly.
  • Semilog reasoning (Math I) — the straightening trick, now automated.
  • Honest reporting (Lesson 2) — precision and units at the interface.

Glossary

TermEquationWhen to use
Line plotclaims continuity/orderTime series and trends — data with a meaningful order.
Scatter plotclaims only observed pairsCorrelation questions; unordered measurements.
Bar chartdiscrete categoriesMachines, months, failure modes — comparisons.
Axis labelsquantity AND unit, alwaysAn unlabeled axis is a meaningless number, plotted.
Semilog (log-y)exponentials → straight linesSlope reads the time constant; the §straightening move.
Log-log / log scalesdecades made visibleSpectra and counts spanning orders of magnitude.
Aliasing\( f_{alias} = |f - f_s| \) (first order)Fast signals masquerading as slow ones in sampled data.

quiz.q:
Choosing the revealing scale

Problem. A casting's cooling data (temperature above ambient vs time) must be tested for first-order behavior and its time constant read graphically. Specify the plot, and what a straight line there proves.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Plot \( \ln(T - T_\infty) \) against t — equivalently, the temperature EXCESS on a semilog (log-y) axis. (2) First-order decay \( e^{-t/\tau} \) becomes a straight line of slope \( -1/\tau \); curvature would refute the single-τ model. (3) Read τ as the negative reciprocal of the fitted slope.

Answer: semilog plot of the excess; straightness proves first-order; slope gives \( \tau \). Sanity check: this is the mathematics course's semilog method, now one plotting call instead of a hand table — same test, same honesty: the plot decides whether the model applies BEFORE the constant is quoted. Scale choice is model testing, not decoration.

quiz.q:
Anatomy of a defensible trend plot

Problem. Specify the complete labeling for a plot of a week of bearing-vibration readings taken every 4 hours — and the plot type, with justification.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Type: LINE plot — the readings are time-ordered, and the claim is a trend. (2) X-axis: "Time (h since Monday 00:00)" — quantity and unit; y-axis: "Vibration velocity (mm/s RMS)". (3) Title naming machine and bearing; if a second bearing shares the axes, a legend naming each series.

Answer: labeled line plot, units on both axes, titled and legended. Sanity check: strip any one element and a real failure mode appears: no units → is that mm/s or in/s?; no title → whose bearing?; scatter instead of line → the trend claim dissolves into dots. The labeling checklist is cheap; the meeting where a mislabeled chart decided wrongly is not.

quiz.q:
The phantom wobble

Problem. A 50 Hz vibration is logged by a system sampling at 60 Hz. Find: the frequency that appears in the data, and state the general caution.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) The sampling can only represent signals up to 30 Hz (half of 60). (2) The 50 Hz signal aliases to \( |50 - 60| = 10\ \mathrm{Hz} \) — a slow, entirely fictitious wobble. (3) Caution: any surprising slow oscillation in sampled data demands the question "what was the sampling rate?" before any diagnosis.

Answer: a phantom 10 Hz signal. Sanity check: the alias is not noise — it is a clean, convincing, WRONG signal, which is what makes it dangerous. The instrumentation course formalizes the Nyquist limit; the plotting lesson's duty is the reflex: sampling rate first, diagnosis second.

quiz.q: Every axis of an engineering plot must carry:
quiz.solution: An axis labeled 'Value' — or nothing — makes the entire figure unfalsifiable: 4.5 of WHAT? The quantity-plus-unit rule is the plotting form of the unit discipline every course here enforces, and reviewers read axes before they read curves. Ticks and trend lines are choices; labels are obligations.

quiz.q: Exponential decay data appears as a straight line on:
quiz.solution: Taking the log of \( Ae^{-t/\tau} \) gives a line in t with slope \( -1/\tau \) — the semilog axis does the logarithm visually. Zooming a linear plot never straightens a curve; it magnifies it. The straightening test is diagnostic, not cosmetic: straight ⇒ first-order applies; curved ⇒ the model (not the plot) needs revisiting — the mathematics course's rule, automated.

quiz.q: To show whether bearing temperature correlates with load across 200 unordered spot readings, the right plot is:
choices: A scatter plot of temperature against load
A line plot in reading order
quiz.solution: The question is pairwise association: temperature VERSUS load, each reading one dot — structure (or its absence) appears as a cloud's shape. A line through unordered readings draws meaningless connections; 200 bars is unreadable; pie charts answer share-of-whole questions only. Type follows question — the first choice of the working method.

quiz.q: Plotting only every 10th point of a dense trend for speed is acceptable when:
choices: The decimation is stated — silently, it can hide spikes
quiz.solution: Decimation is a legitimate rendering economy with a failure mode: a one-sample spike — the very event a review hunts — can fall between kept points and vanish. Stated ("plotted at 1/10 resolution; extrema verified separately"), the reader knows the contract; silent, the plot testifies to smoothness it never checked. Honesty here is disclosure, as everywhere.

quiz.q: A bar chart comparing monthly downtime hours should generally have its y-axis:
choices: Starting at zero, so bar heights compare honestly
Starting just below the smallest value, to show detail
quiz.solution: Bar charts encode value as LENGTH, and lengths compare honestly only from a common zero — a truncated baseline turns a 5% difference into a visual 3:1 ratio, the oldest chart trick in the persuasion business. (Line plots may zoom, with axes labeled, since they encode position, not length.) The skeptic's-eye pass of the working method looks for exactly this.
